Actually, the user said "I just need the email text." This is a clear, simple drafting request. I should just deliver the email. Doing a document search would be over-engineering and ignoring the user's explicit instruction.

Let me draft the email:

Paragraph 1: Polite opening, propose the one-week extension, brief reason (need a bit more time for review).
Paragraph 2: Acknowledge any inconvenience, offer to discuss, sign off politely.

Let me write it:

---

Subject: Request for One-Week Extension — Contract Review

Dear [Counsel's Name],

I hope this message finds you well. I'm writing to request a brief extension of our current deadline for the contract review — specifically, an additional week to allow our team the time needed to complete a thorough review. We want to ensure that our response is both careful and constructive, and the additional time would help us achieve that.

I apologize for any inconvenience this may cause, and I'm happy to discuss the revised timeline at your convenience. Thank you for your understanding, and I look forward to hearing from you.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

---

That's clean, two paragraphs (plus sign-off), polite, generic, and doesn't reference any specific agreement. I'll deliver this directly without unnecessary tool calls.
</think>

Subject: Request for a One-Week Extension — Contract Review

Dear [Opposing Counsel],

I hope you're doing well. I'm writing to request a short extension of our current deadline for the contract review — specifically, an additional week — so that our team has the time needed to complete a thorough and careful review. We want to make sure our response is constructive, and the extra time would help us get there.

I apologize for any inconvenience this may cause and am happy to discuss a revised timeline at your convenience. Thank you for your understanding, and please don't hesitate to reach out if you'd like to talk it through.

Best regards,
[Your Name]
